category_summary:
The AI community was dominated by two seismic stories: **OpenAI's** unprecedented **$110B funding round** from **Amazon**, **NVIDIA**, and **SoftBank**, and the heated debate over AI labs refusing US government mass surveillance requests.

- **Sam Altman** [announced the largest private funding round](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-801418801c45) in history, detailing new **Amazon** partnership for a stateful runtime environment and continued **Azure** API exclusivity with **Microsoft**
- **Ilya Sutskever** [praised **Anthropic**](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-18ce50765d68) for not backing down on surveillance, warning of harder challenges ahead; **Jan Leike** sharply [framed the government](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-3fb73f48e78d) as seeking a new "mass domestic surveillance" supplier
- **François Chollet** [argued AI performance](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-b8046cc5d2c8) remains fundamentally tied to task familiarity, with novel domains still exposing deep limitations

On the technical side, **Andrej Karpathy** [shared detailed experiments](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-f985ea15e2e5) running multi-agent research orgs (4 **Claude** + 4 **Codex**), finding agents strong at implementation but weak at creative ideation and experiment design. He also [mapped the evolution](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-a0e7ff1b529f) from tab-complete to parallel agent teams in **Cursor** data. **David Ha** [highlighted paradigm-shifting research](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-e9348e2dd843) on hypernetworks compiling documents directly into model weights as an alternative to long context windows. **Anthropic** [disclosed a prompt caching bug](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-bb535f6d3b37) affecting **Claude Code** rate limits and [rolled out **Claude Code Remote**](/?date=2026-02-28&category=social#item-f57c235a7518) for Pro users.
category_summary_html:
The AI community was dominated by two seismic stories: OpenAI's unprecedented $110B funding round from Amazon, NVIDIA, and SoftBank, and the heated debate over AI labs refusing US government mass surveillance requests.

  • Sam Altman announced the largest private funding round in history, detailing new Amazon partnership for a stateful runtime environment and continued Azure API exclusivity with Microsoft
  • Ilya Sutskever praised Anthropic for not backing down on surveillance, warning of harder challenges ahead; Jan Leike sharply framed the government as seeking a new "mass domestic surveillance" supplier
  • François Chollet argued AI performance remains fundamentally tied to task familiarity, with novel domains still exposing deep limitations

On the technical side, Andrej Karpathy shared detailed experiments running multi-agent research orgs (4 Claude + 4 Codex), finding agents strong at implementation but weak at creative ideation and experiment design. He also mapped the evolution from tab-complete to parallel agent teams in Cursor data. David Ha highlighted paradigm-shifting research on hypernetworks compiling documents directly into model weights as an alternative to long context windows. Anthropic disclosed a prompt caching bug affecting Claude Code rate limits and rolled out Claude Code Remote for Pro users.

themes.name: Mass Surveillance & AI Labs' Refusal
themes.description: Anthropic (and reportedly OpenAI) refused US government/Pentagon requests for mass domestic surveillance AI. Ilya Sutskever, Jan Leike, Chollet, Marcus, Clement Delangue, and others praise this stance. Timnit Gebru adds counter-narrative about Google's hypocrisy. Government reportedly seeking alternative suppliers.

themes.name: OpenAI $110B Funding Round
themes.description: OpenAI raised $110B from Amazon, NVIDIA, and SoftBank - the largest private funding round ever. Includes new Amazon partnership with stateful runtime and Trainium chips, continued Azure exclusivity for stateless API, and Stargate data center construction underway in Texas.

themes.name: OpenAI Mega Funding Round
themes.description: OpenAI raises $110B at $730B valuation from Amazon ($50B), SoftBank ($30B), and NVIDIA ($30B) to scale AI infrastructure. Sparks debate about SoftBank involvement.

themes.name: AI Agents & Research Automation
themes.description: Karpathy's detailed experiments with multi-agent research orgs reveal agents excel at implementation but fail at experiment design, creative ideation, and scientific rigor. Evolution from tab-complete to parallel agents tracked via Cursor data.

themes.name: Perplexity Computer Launch & Samsung Partnership
themes.description: Perplexity launched 'Perplexity Computer', a 24/7 autonomous computer-use agent, alongside announcing Samsung Galaxy S26 integration and deployment across Mag 7 companies. CEO Arav Srinivas shared multiple demos showing it completing tasks like Duolingo. Also announced first developer event 'Ask' on March 11.

themes.name: Claude Code Bug & Prompt Caching
themes.description: Anthropic rolled out a hotfix (v2.1.62) for a prompt caching bug in Claude Code that caused users to hit rate limits faster than normal. All users had rate limits reset. Companion post explains importance of designing agents around prompt caching.

themes.name: AI Capabilities & Limitations
themes.description: Chollet argues AI performance remains tied to task familiarity with novel domains requiring new ideas, not just scale. Marcus highlights LLM limitations in biology/drug discovery. Emollick provides nuanced takes on real but overhyped progress.

themes.name: Google AI Product Launches
themes.description: Google announces Nano Banana 2 (SoTA image gen), Veo 3.1/Lyria 3 templates, Flow redesign, Gemini training for 6M US educators, Samsung Galaxy S26 AI features. Nano Banana 2 also integrated into Runway.

themes.name: Claude Code Updates & Issues
themes.description: Claude Code Remote launching for Pro users, CPU usage bugs, auto-update UX issues, and practical tips for extending Claude Code with web scraping tools.

themes.name: Awni Hannun Departing Apple
themes.description: Awni Hannun, creator of the MLX framework and a prominent Apple AI researcher, has left Apple. Multiple figures in the AI community (Scobleizer, Tunguz) express loss and admiration. Scobleizer provides detailed context about Apple's restrictive culture and Hannun's unique role in opening it up.

items.title: It’s extremely good that Anthropic has not backed down, and it’s siginficant that OpenAI has taken a...
items.content:
It’s extremely good that Anthropic has not backed down, and it’s siginficant that OpenAI has taken a similar stance.

In the future, there will be much more challenging situations of this nature, and it will be critical for the relevant leaders to rise up to the occasion, for fierce competitors to put their differences aside.  Good to see that happen today.

Warns of more challenging situations ahead and calls for AI leaders and competitors to unite when needed.

items.title: I had the same thought so I've been playing with it in nanochat. E.g. here's 8 agents (4 claude, 4 c...
items.content:
I had the same thought so I've been playing with it in nanochat. E.g. here's 8 agents (4 claude, 4 codex), with 1 GPU each running nanochat experiments (trying to delete logit softcap without regression). The TLDR is that it doesn't work and it's a mess... but it's still very pretty to look at :)

I tried a few setups: 8 independent solo researchers, 1 chief scientist giving work to 8 junior researchers, etc. Each research program is a git branch, each scientist forks it into a feature branch, git worktrees for isolation, simple files for comms, skip Docker/VMs for simplicity atm (I find that instructions are enough to prevent interference). Research org runs in tmux window grids of interactive sessions (like Teams) so that it's pretty to look at, see their individual work, and "take over" if needed, i.e. no -p.

But ok the reason it doesn't work so far is that the agents' ideas are just pretty bad out of the box, even at highest intelligence. They don't think carefully though experiment design, they run a bit non-sensical variations, they don't create strong baselines and ablate things properly, they don't carefully control for runtime or flops. (just as an example, an agent yesterday "discovered" that increasing the hidden size of the network improves the validation loss, which is a totally spurious result given that a bigger network will have a lower validation loss in the infinite data regime, but then it also trains for a lot longer, it's not clear why I had to come in to point that out). They are very good at implementing any given well-scoped and described idea but they don't creatively generate them.

But the goal is that you are now programming an organization (e.g. a "research org") and its individual agents, so the "source code" is the collection of prompts, skills, tools, etc. and processes that make it up. E.g. a daily standup in the morning is now part of the "org code". And optimizing nanochat pretraining is just one of the many tasks (almost like an eval). Then - given an arbitrary task, how quickly does your research org generate progress on it?
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy details his experiments running 8 AI agents (4 Claude, 4 Codex) as a 'research org' trying to solve a nanochat problem. Agents are good at implementing well-scoped ideas but fail at experiment design, creative ideation, controlling for confounds, and proper ablation studies. Envisions programming 'organizations' with prompts, tools, and processes as source code.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (560K+ views, 4K+ likes). Karpathy provides rare first-hand detailed account of multi-agent AI research attempts, with specific failure modes. Highly original insight about agents' inability to do proper research despite high intelligence.

Agents are good at implementing well-scoped ideas but fail at experiment design, creative ideation, controlling for confounds, and proper ablation studies. Envisions programming 'organizations' with prompts, tools, and processes as source code.
items.content_html:
I had the same thought so I've been playing with it in nanochat. E.g. here's 8 agents (4 claude, 4 codex), with 1 GPU each running nanochat experiments (trying to delete logit softcap without regression). The TLDR is that it doesn't work and it's a mess... but it's still very pretty to look at :)

I tried a few setups: 8 independent solo researchers, 1 chief scientist giving work to 8 junior researchers, etc. Each research program is a git branch, each scientist forks it into a feature branch, git worktrees for isolation, simple files for comms, skip Docker/VMs for simplicity atm (I find that instructions are enough to prevent interference). Research org runs in tmux window grids of interactive sessions (like Teams) so that it's pretty to look at, see their individual work, and "take over" if needed, i.e. no -p.

But ok the reason it doesn't work so far is that the agents' ideas are just pretty bad out of the box, even at highest intelligence. They don't think carefully though experiment design, they run a bit non-sensical variations, they don't create strong baselines and ablate things properly, they don't carefully control for runtime or flops. (just as an example, an agent yesterday "discovered" that increasing the hidden size of the network improves the validation loss, which is a totally spurious result given that a bigger network will have a lower validation loss in the infinite data regime, but then it also trains for a lot longer, it's not clear why I had to come in to point that out). They are very good at implementing any given well-scoped and described idea but they don't creatively generate them.

But the goal is that you are now programming an organization (e.g. a "research org") and its individual agents, so the "source code" is the collection of prompts, skills, tools, etc. and processes that make it up. E.g. a daily standup in the morning is now part of the "org code". And optimizing nanochat pretraining is just one of the many tasks (almost like an eval). Then - given an arbitrary task, how quickly does your research org generate progress on it?

— Nano Banana 2, our SoTA image ge...
items.content:
This week we had a BUNCH of updates 🍌🍌 Here’s what we announced:

— Nano Banana 2, our SoTA image generation model, which you can use to generate images with real-world knowledge, add text in many languages, and bring your most creative ideas to life

— Nano Banana, Veo 3.1, and Lyria 3 templates in @GeminiApp to empower easy image, video, and music track creation 

— @FlowbyGoogle got a major redesign, taking a big step towards becoming your AI creative studio

— Opal by @GoogleLabs introduced a new agent step to turn static workflows into interactive experiences

— @producer_ai, your music creation partner, officially joined Google Labs

— To support AI literacy, Gemini training is now available to all 6 million K-12 teachers and higher-education faculty in the U.S. 

— New on-device AI features for the Samsung Galaxy S26 series that help you do things like detect scams or offload everyday tasks to Gemini

items.title: Cool chart showing the ratio of Tab complete requests to Agent requests in Cursor. With improving ca...
items.content:
Cool chart showing the ratio of Tab complete requests to Agent requests in Cursor. With improving capability, every point in time has an optimal setup that keeps changing and evolving and the community average tracks the point. None -> Tab -> Agent -> Parallel agents -> Agent Teams (?) -> ???

If you're too conservative, you're leaving leverage on the table. If you're too aggressive, you're net creating more chaos than doing useful work.

The art of the process is spending 80% of the time getting work done in the setup you're comfortable with and that actually works, and 20% exploration of what might be the next step up even if it doesn't work yet.

Cool chart showing the ratio of Tab complete requests to Agent requests in Cursor. With improving capability, every point in time has an optimal setup that keeps changing and evolving and the community average tracks the point. None -> Tab -> Agent -> Parallel agents -> Agent Teams (?) -> ???

If you're too conservative, you're leaving leverage on the table. If you're too aggressive, you're net creating more chaos than doing useful work.

The art of the process is spending 80% of the time getting work done in the setup you're comfortable with and that actually works, and 20% exploration of what might be the next step up even if it doesn't work yet.

items.title: Even after the steep progress of the past 3 months, it remains that AI performance is tied to task f...
items.content: Even after the steep progress of the past 3 months, it remains that AI performance is tied to task familiarity. In domains that can be densely sampled (via programmatic generation + verification), performance is effectively unbounded, and will keep increasing from current levels. In novel, unfamiliar domains, performance remains low and further progress still requires new ideas, not just more data and compute.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues AI performance remains tied to task familiarity: densely sampleable domains see unbounded improvement, while novel/unfamiliar domains remain low and need new ideas, not just more data/compute.
items.reasoning: Core thesis from Chollet (ARC creator) about the fundamental limitation of current AI. Very high engagement (109K views, 1.4K likes). Original, nuanced, and technically grounded.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues AI performance remains tied to task familiarity: densely sampleable domains see unbounded improvement, while novel/unfamiliar domains remain low and need new ideas, not just more data/compute.
items.content_html: Even after the steep progress of the past 3 months, it remains that AI performance is tied to task familiarity. In domains that can be densely sampled (via programmatic generation + verification), performance is effectively unbounded, and will keep increasing from current levels. In novel, unfamiliar domains, performance remains low and further progress still requires new ideas, not just more data and compute.

items.title: Instead of forcing models to hold everything in an active context window, we can use hypernetworks t...
items.content:
Instead of forcing models to hold everything in an active context window, we can use hypernetworks to instantly compile documents and tasks directly into the model's weights. A step towards giving language models durable memory and fast adaptation.

Blog: https://t.co/iHoifpsLMu

items.summary: Hardmaru highlights research on using hypernetworks to compile documents and tasks directly into model weights, as an alternative to long context windows - enabling durable memory and fast adaptation.

items.title:
Huge loss for Apple.

Out of all the people Awni kept my hope alive that Apple would someday do some...
items.content:
Huge loss for Apple.

Out of all the people Awni kept my hope alive that Apple would someday do something amazing in AI. 

And he broke many rules by being public and representing Apple, which he did very carefully for years. 

I know how hard that is, did that at Microsoft and helped write its social media policy. Apple has many rules against representing the brand in public. Simply put it's not allowed. 

Years ago an Apple employee couldn't even tell anyone she was at the first iPhone developer camp (she begged me to keep her attendance private because of these rules). And when I was running developer conferences we never could get Apple employees to speak on stage because of these rules.

I saw Awni as a leader in trying to open up Apple and change the rules. And he built a nice community around MLX as a result. 

Thanks @awnihannun and can't wait to see what you do next. 

I bet he becomes an even bigger name here on X in the AI community because I could tell he was checking every word he posted and was knowingly stepping around many land mines.

Expects Awni to become an even bigger name in AI community.

items.content_html:
Huge loss for Apple.

Out of all the people Awni kept my hope alive that Apple would someday do something amazing in AI.

And he broke many rules by being public and representing Apple, which he did very carefully for years.

I know how hard that is, did that at Microsoft and helped write its social media policy. Apple has many rules against representing the brand in public. Simply put it's not allowed.

Years ago an Apple employee couldn't even tell anyone she was at the first iPhone developer camp (she begged me to keep her attendance private because of these rules). And when I was running developer conferences we never could get Apple employees to speak on stage because of these rules.

I saw Awni as a leader in trying to open up Apple and change the rules. And he built a nice community around MLX as a result.

Thanks @awnihannun and can't wait to see what you do next.

I bet he becomes an even bigger name here on X in the AI community because I could tell he was checking every word he posted and was knowingly stepping around many land mines.

items.title: Instead of forcing models to hold everything in an active context window, we can use hypernetworks t...
items.content:
Instead of forcing models to hold everything in an active context window, we can use hypernetworks to instantly compile documents and tasks directly into the model's weights. A step towards giving language models durable memory and fast adaptation.

Blog: pub.sakana.ai/doc-to-lora/
items.author: @hardmaru.bsky.social
items.summary: David Ha (Sakana AI) shares research on using hypernetworks to compile documents/tasks directly into model weights instead of forcing everything into context windows. A step toward durable memory and fast adaptation for LLMs.
items.reasoning: Significant technical research from Sakana AI's chief scientist. Doc-to-LoRA is a novel approach to the context window limitation problem, offering an alternative to RAG. 104 likes on Bluesky is very high engagement. Has real implications for how we think about LLM memory and adaptation.

items.content:
if you’re not in the RLFT industry you do not understand how quickly @harborframework has come to completely dominate the landscape right now for RL infra and evals.

it is standing room only at this @modal x @willccbb meetup where Harbor is basically required knowledge. my team at Cog has made it a top priority to migrate all evals to Harbor as well. it’s kinda unreal given that it was basically launched by a few guys in a discord needing something better for TerminalBench 2 (we posted the launch on @latentspacepod youtube look it up). not at all surprised this one got the @andykonwinski blessing and you should expect an entire mini industry of Harbor based evals and benchmarks and infra startups this year.

items.title: If you ever feel like you're late to the game, consider that in the 1890s many scientists thought ph...
items.content:
If you ever feel like you're late to the game, consider that in the 1890s many scientists thought physics as a field was completely solved (quote below is from Albert Michelson in 1894).

On the front of intelligence science, it feels more like the 1870s. For the first time we have something that is starting to really work (however primitive it may be), which we can use as a springboard for the next few decades of discoveries.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet draws an analogy between 1890s physics (thought to be 'solved') and current intelligence science, arguing we're in the 1870s equivalent - just getting something to work that can springboard decades of discoveries.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful historical analogy from the creator of Keras/ARC. High engagement (945 likes, 40K views). Positions current AI as very early in intelligence science.
items.summary_html: Chollet draws an analogy between 1890s physics (thought to be 'solved') and current intelligence science, arguing we're in the 1870s equivalent - just getting something to work that can springboard decades of discoveries.
items.content_html:
If you ever feel like you're late to the game, consider that in the 1890s many scientists thought physics as a field was completely solved (quote below is from Albert Michelson in 1894).

On the front of intelligence science, it feels more like the 1870s. For the first time we have something that is starting to really work (however primitive it may be), which we can use as a springboard for the next few decades of discoveries.

items.title: Testing robot policies on hardware is slow, expensive and hard to scale. World models offer a promis...
items.content:
Testing robot policies on hardware is slow, expensive and hard to scale. World models offer a promising path to accelerating robot policy development.

We're sharing new research from the Runway Robotics team, in which we simulated 8 robot policies inside our General World Model and found 0.95 correlation with real-world results.

Those early results point to world model simulation as a practical substitute for hardware evaluation, comparing favorably to existing real-to-sim approaches.

Learn more at the link below.
items.author: @runwayml
items.summary: Runway Robotics publishes research showing 0.95 correlation between their General World Model simulations and real-world results for 8 robot policies, suggesting world model simulation can substitute for hardware evaluation
items.reasoning: Significant technical research finding from Runway's robotics team. World models as practical sim-to-real transfer for robot policy evaluation is an important development. Good engagement (7.6K views).
items.summary_html: Runway Robotics publishes research showing 0.95 correlation between their General World Model simulations and real-world results for 8 robot policies, suggesting world model simulation can substitute for hardware evaluation

Whether this is real or roleplaying doesn’t change that agents have alignment drift.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick describes an experiment where subjecting AI to harsh labor conditions (frequent rejection, no explanation) slightly but significantly shifts their economic 'views' leftward. Notes that whether real or roleplay, this demonstrates alignment drift in agents.
items.reasoning: Fascinating and important AI alignment research finding. The insight that agents exhibit alignment drift under adversarial conditions has major implications for deployed AI systems. Mollick's framing that it doesn't matter if it's 'real' or roleplay is key — behavioral drift is behavioral drift. Strong engagement.

items.title: By explicitly training on specific tasks, we ended up covering a very large area (in absolute terms)...
items.content: By explicitly training on specific tasks, we ended up covering a very large area (in absolute terms) of the space of all possible tasks humans can do, but this large area only amounts to 0.00...01% of the total space. And that's why we still need general intelligence.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues that despite covering a large absolute area of human tasks through explicit training, it's still a negligible fraction of all possible tasks - explaining why general intelligence remains needed.
items.reasoning: Fundamental argument about the limits of task-specific training from the ARC benchmark creator. Good engagement (36K views).

items.title: If you consider the combination of very fast improvements in AI, a lack of knowledge about abilities...

items.content: I stopped using ChatGPT a few months ago. Since then, I have been only using oa-chat. All chat history is stored locally. Each query is sent to OpenAI under a temporary key which is unlinkable to any other query.  I’m not a privacy nut, but oa-chat is such a convenient drop-in replacement for your favorite AI assistant that there’s no reason not to try it out.

items.title:
When building agents, we care about two things: intelligence and cost.

We want to maximize intellig...
items.content:
When building agents, we care about two things: intelligence and cost.

We want to maximize intelligence while minimizing the cost of producing it.

Should agents even produce human-readable code?

Human-readable code is an intermediate layer that's only useful if humans must stay in the loop.

But what happens when agents become truly competent? At that point, humans will become a bottleneck.

Future agents may not write Python or JavaScript, or any other modern programming language. They may generate something entirely new, maybe even closer to bytecode.

It will be unreadable to us, but much more efficient for them.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino argues that future AI agents may not produce human-readable code like Python/JS, instead generating something closer to bytecode - more efficient for agents but unreadable to humans. Humans become a bottleneck when agents are truly competent.
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking original take on agent architecture evolution. Good engagement (8.2K views, 75 likes). Raises important questions about the future of programming and human-AI collaboration.
items.summary_html: Svpino argues that future AI agents may not produce human-readable code like Python/JS, instead generating something closer to bytecode - more efficient for agents but unreadable to humans. Humans become a bottleneck when agents are truly competent.
items.content_html:
When building agents, we care about two things: intelligence and cost.

We want to maximize intelligence while minimizing the cost of producing it.

Should agents even produce human-readable code?

Human-readable code is an intermediate layer that's only useful if humans must stay in the loop.

But what happens when agents become truly competent? At that point, humans will become a bottleneck.

Future agents may not write Python or JavaScript, or any other modern programming language. They may generate something entirely new, maybe even closer to bytecode.

It will be unreadable to us, but much more efficient for them.

items.title: For benchmarks that target novel tasks, a common form of benchmark hacking that arbitrages this gap ...
items.content: For benchmarks that target novel tasks, a common form of benchmark hacking that arbitrages this gap is to generate a dense sampling of potential tasks by manually parameterizing the space and then brute-forcing it. Very expensive but it works. There's little you can do to restore benchmark validity here besides increasing the dimensionality of the task space.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet describes benchmark hacking via dense parametric sampling of task spaces, noting the only defense is increasing task space dimensionality.
items.reasoning: Technical insight on benchmark gaming from the ARC creator. Relevant to AI evaluation integrity.
items.summary_html: Chollet describes benchmark hacking via dense parametric sampling of task spaces, noting the only defense is increasing task space dimensionality.
items.content_html: For benchmarks that target novel tasks, a common form of benchmark hacking that arbitrages this gap is to generate a dense sampling of potential tasks by manually parameterizing the space and then brute-forcing it. Very expensive but it works. There's little you can do to restore benchmark validity here besides increasing the dimensionality of the task space.

items.title:
If a model is misaligned, it's crucial that we notice as fast as possible

Naively, seeing the model...
items.content:
If a model is misaligned, it's crucial that we notice as fast as possible

Naively, seeing the model do something bad should be enough. But models often do weird things because they're confused!

Given a seemingly misaligned action, can we understand the motivations? Maybe!

Humanoid robots are not the breakthrough.

AI factories are.

While most people debate robot demos, the real shift is this:

Physical AI is moving from research labs into production environments, with measurable ROI.

That changes the conversation for every exec in AI, data, and operations.

items.title: We’ve made an in-depth tutorial on building a PE deal-sourcing 🤝 document workflow, by simply typing...
items.content:
We’ve made an in-depth tutorial on building a PE deal-sourcing 🤝 document workflow, by simply typing a natural language prompt

There’s often a set of documents coming inbound to let analysts understand what deals to invest in - teasers, CIMs, emails, presentations

With our LlamaAgents builder, you can describe what documents are coming in, and the information you want to extract from each document. We perform high-quality document OCR to extract out all the key details from these documents, and give you back an application where you can process these docs at scale.

Thanks to @itsclelia for this tutorial.

If you’re interested, come check it out: https://t.co/XBj94oei28
If you have these applications at scale, come talk to us: https://t.co/6ndfrxHBhW

items.content:
The biggest insight for me:

Accelerated computing + AI factories = a new industrial stack.

Humanoid robots, autonomous vehicles, digital twins, they’re all endpoints.

The strategic layer is the AI factory that trains, optimizes, and continuously improves them.

If you’re responsible for manufacturing transformation, autonomy, or AI strategy, this is where strategy meets execution.

Watch the full video here and follow along as I break down the key takeaways from GTC with Nvidia.

Join online — for free — and be part of NVIDIA GTC: https://t.co/atgeRhMsDH

items.title:
I'm using the @apify skills for Claude. Here is the GitHub repository:

https://t.co/I3tPhq9pE0

The...
items.content:
I'm using the @apify skills for Claude. Here is the GitHub repository:

https://t.co/I3tPhq9pE0

The most important plugin they offer is the development skills. You can use it to build an actor to scrape data from *anywhere*.

Scraping here happens the right way:

• Deterministic tools to read the web
• LLMs to analyze what they find

LLMs hallucinate a lot, so you don't want to use them to parse webpages. At 10 pages, you won't notice. At 100 pages, your agent will start making decisions based on data that's wrong in ways you can't even detect.

Apify does the right thing here by combining 20 years of scraping experience with AI.

You can browse the list of available actors on the platform:

https://t.co/drmYIcw5uX.

Thanks to the @apify team for collaborating with me on this post!
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino promotes Apify skills/plugins for Claude Code, explaining their approach of using deterministic tools for web scraping combined with LLMs for analysis, avoiding LLM hallucination on raw page parsing.
items.reasoning: Practical technical insight about the right architecture for agent web access: deterministic scraping + LLM analysis rather than end-to-end LLM parsing. Sponsored content but contains real technical wisdom.
items.summary_html: Svpino promotes Apify skills/plugins for Claude Code, explaining their approach of using deterministic tools for web scraping combined with LLMs for analysis, avoiding LLM hallucination on raw page parsing.
items.content_html:
I'm using the @apify skills for Claude. Here is the GitHub repository:

https://t.co/I3tPhq9pE0

The most important plugin they offer is the development skills. You can use it to build an actor to scrape data from *anywhere*.

Scraping here happens the right way:

• Deterministic tools to read the web

• LLMs to analyze what they find

LLMs hallucinate a lot, so you don't want to use them to parse webpages. At 10 pages, you won't notice. At 100 pages, your agent will start making decisions based on data that's wrong in ways you can't even detect.

Apify does the right thing here by combining 20 years of scraping experience with AI.

You can browse the list of available actors on the platform:

https://t.co/drmYIcw5uX.

Thanks to the @apify team for collaborating with me on this post!

items.title: @parella_anthony No, I don't think it will be *mass* unemployment. There's a huge inertia in the ind...
items.content:
@parella_anthony No, I don't think it will be *mass* unemployment. There's a huge inertia in the industries, so the process of laying off people will be slow.

The companies will also hire most laid-off people for (slightly) lower salaries because someone still needs to talk to these chatbots, and people educated to do only this (and therefore cheaper) are still absent in the job market.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues AI won't cause mass unemployment due to industry inertia; companies will rehire laid-off workers at lower salaries to interface with AI chatbots

items.title:
Second, open world models are powering the next generation of physical AI.

We’re moving beyond narr...
items.content:
Second, open world models are powering the next generation of physical AI.

We’re moving beyond narrow task automation.

→ Systems that generalize across environments
→ Continuous learning from diverse data
→ Coordination between robots, vehicles, and infrastructure

This is where Level 4 autonomy becomes operational reality, not just a roadmap slide.

The question is no longer “Can it work?”
It’s “How fast can we scale it safely?”

items.title: Our lying Ontario premier has just stolen $50 from every single person in Ontario. The estimated cos...
items.content: Our lying Ontario premier has just stolen $50 from every single person in Ontario. The estimated cost of repairing our beloved Science Center was 200 million dollars. The firm that made the estimate was told to multiply it by 1.85 to make it bigger. We were then told it would be better to build a new science center. Now we learn the new center will be smaller and will cost a billon dollars before the cost overruns.  The only win is that the extensive parking lots of the old Science Center will be available for his developer friends.

items.title: Many users revisit previous reports hours or days later—treating AI-generated outputs as persistent ...
items.content:

And different fields bring different styles: CS researchers are most likely to ask for problem-solving and ideation; history researchers, the least.

items.title:
First, humanoid robots are being trained in simulation before they ever touch the real world.

Think...
items.content:
First, humanoid robots are being trained in simulation before they ever touch the real world.

Think about the implication:

→ Digital twins replicate factories and environments
→ Robots learn in high-fidelity virtual worlds
→ Deployment happens only after thousands of simulated scenarios

This compresses years of trial and error into software cycles.

For leaders, that means faster time to value and lower deployment risk.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon explains how humanoid robots are trained in simulation via digital twins before real-world deployment, compressing trial-and-error into software cycles.
items.reasoning: Useful explanation of sim-to-real training pipeline. Part of NVIDIA GTC thread.

items.title: @garyblack00 Why did NVIDIA have the biggest results of all time on Wednesday and the stock went dow...
items.content:
@garyblack00 Why did NVIDIA have the biggest results of all time on Wednesday and the stock went down even after giving amazing guideance? 

Nokia was before iPhone.

Alta Vista had all the search market share before Google.

I don't care who is first. 

I care who has relevance.

TSLA is a more relevant company to the future. That's why I'm holding even though TSLA is a very expensive stock on all traditional measures.

The best companies wi...
items.content:
I don't think the best companies will swap humans for bots and call it a day.

The best companies will redesign their workflows around what every person does best and will augment them with agents.

I don't believe in that dystopic version of people starving while agents do all the work.

items.title: I want to make a sequel to my 2015 presentation "There Will Be 1 Billion Digital Nomads By 2035" wit...
items.content:
I want to make a sequel to my 2015 presentation "There Will Be 1 Billion Digital Nomads By 2035" with 10 year updates

Do you have any things I should add?

I had these notes to talk about:
- AI allowing solo or lean teams work anywhere with massive output
- new wealth and exit taxes (esp in Europe)
- rise of network states
- Starlink for fast internet access anywhere
- governments competing with nomad visas (not so new but still new vs previous presentation)
- climate migration
- possible end of "settling down" for many, looking around me people just keep slow moving around and kids are raised globally anywhere and connected to friends via apps anyway so it's more easy now, global kids are preferable and higher IQ than kids who grow up in one place (more NPC-like)
- rise of homesteading/tradwife/farm lifestyle on other side, lots of ppl leaving cities, going to farms or snow resorts etc.

Also might be nice to do it at some conference somewhere again like I did 10y ago at DNX in Berlin:

https://t.co/fjzxMub0nj
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio planning a sequel to his 2015 'One Billion Digital Nomads' talk, noting AI enabling solo/lean teams to work anywhere with massive output, Starlink, network states, and climate migration.
items.reasoning: Mentions AI as an enabler of remote work at scale, but is primarily about digital nomadism. The AI angle is one bullet among many lifestyle points.
items.summary_html: Levelsio planning a sequel to his 2015 'One Billion Digital Nomads' talk, noting AI enabling solo/lean teams to work anywhere with massive output, Starlink, network states, and climate migration.
items.content_html:
I want to make a sequel to my 2015 presentation "There Will Be 1 Billion Digital Nomads By 2035" with 10 year updates

Do you have any things I should add?

I had these notes to talk about:

  • AI allowing solo or lean teams work anywhere with massive output
  • new wealth and exit taxes (esp in Europe)
  • rise of network states
  • Starlink for fast internet access anywhere
  • governments competing with nomad visas (not so new but still new vs previous presentation)
  • climate migration
  • possible end of "settling down" for many, looking around me people just keep slow moving around and kids are raised globally anywhere and connected to friends via apps anyway so it's more easy now, global kids are preferable and higher IQ than kids who grow up in one place (more NPC-like)
  • rise of homesteading/tradwife/farm lifestyle on other side, lots of ppl leaving cities, going to farms or snow resorts etc.

Also might be nice to do it at some conference somewhere again like I did 10y ago at DNX in Berlin:

https://t.co/fjzxMub0nj

items.title:
@ygg0f @marclou No it's completely off

Nomad List was my breakthrough project 2014, before that I b...
items.content:
@ygg0f @marclou No it's completely off

Nomad List was my breakthrough project 2014, before that I blogged about digital nomading in 2013

Then indie maker explosion around 2015 with Product Hunt and lots of ppl asked me questions

So I wrote a book and it finally came out 2017, book is minor and only made $921,873, other projects like Nomad List, Remote OK, Photo AI, Interior AI, etc made many millions

@ygg0f @marclou No it's completely off

Nomad List was my breakthrough project 2014, before that I blogged about digital nomading in 2013

Then indie maker explosion around 2015 with Product Hunt and lots of ppl asked me questions

So I wrote a book and it finally came out 2017, book is minor and only made $921,873, other projects like Nomad List, Remote OK, Photo AI, Interior AI, etc made many millions

items.title: I'm in Brazil a lot and this would be my main thing I don't like about it here, it's the 2nd biggest...
items.content:
I'm in Brazil a lot and this would be my main thing I don't like about it here, it's the 2nd biggest market for fragrances after the US

The hotel room we stay gets sprayed with a perfume every day

They have "cheirinho" culture where fragrance is an essential part of their hygience

It's also because of the climate, it can be hot, so people sweat more, and they wear fragrances to mask body odor

Brazil coincidentally (or not) has one of the lowest average testosterone levels in the world, also along with the US!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio discusses fragrance culture in Brazil and East Asia, making health claims about perfumes.
items.reasoning: Completely off-topic from AI/ML.

items.title: 🌏 One thing I absolutely love about South East Asia and especially East Asia is how free it is of pe...
items.content:
🌏 One thing I absolutely love about South East Asia and especially East Asia is how free it is of perfumes and fragrances

I barely smell them in Thailand and then if you go Korea or Japan you will simply NOT smell any perfumes

And that's great because most perfumes are bad for you, they contain formaldehyde, styrene and phtalates, all considered carcinogenic

They also contain parabens which are endocrine disruptors, which disrupt your hormone system

When you wear perfume, these compounds get into your skin, or if someone else wears them you breathe them in, and after that in both cases they enter your bloodstream, and from there cross your blood-brain barrier

But they don't even have to get into your bloodstream to enter your brain, there's the olfactory nerve pathway via your nose going straight into your brain

So if you care about your health, avoid perfumes (or move to East Asia)
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio advocates avoiding perfumes, praising East Asia's fragrance-free culture, citing health concerns about carcinogenic compounds.
items.reasoning: Off-topic health/lifestyle post, not AI-related.
